query: What are the projected sea level rise impacts on Venice specifically, including flood frequency projections and MOSE barrier effectiveness under different IPCC scenarios?
synthesis:
Venice faces accelerating Relative Sea-Level Rise (RSLR) and increasing flood frequency, threatening the long-term effectiveness of the Mo.S.E. (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) barrier system. Historical RSLR has averaged 2.5 mm/year over the last 150 years, but tide-gauge data show recent acceleration to 2.76 mm/year (1993–2019) [Sea-level rise in Venice: historic and future trends (review article)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/). Current regional tide levels are rising at approximately 0.5 cm per year, though localized rates at lagoon inlets reach up to 5 mm/year due to subsidence [New study by Italian scientists predicts Venice will be underwater by 2150 | Euronews](https://www.euronews.com/2024/06/19/new-study-by-italian-scientists-predicts-venice-will-be-underwater-by-2150).

### IPCC Scenario Projections and Inundation Risks
Under IPCC AR6 scenarios, projected RSLR for Venice by 2100 varies significantly:
- **Low Emissions (SSP1-2.6/RCP 2.6):** 28–62 cm [Sea-level rise in Venice: historic and future trends (review article)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/) [New study by Italian scientists predicts Venice will be underwater by 2150 | Euronews](https://www.euronews.com/2024/06/19/new-study-by-italian-scientists-predicts-venice-will-be-underwater-by-2150).
- **High Emissions (SSP5-8.5/RCP 8.5):** 58–110 cm, with high-end plausible scenarios reaching 180 cm [Sea-level rise in Venice: historic and future trends (review article)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/). Extreme models suggest up to 2.5m by 2100 under RCP 8.5 if ice-sheet melting is included [Sea Level Rise Projection Map - Venice | Earth.Org](https://earth.org/data_visualization/sea-level-rise-by-the-end-of-the-century-venice/).
- **Long-term (2150):** RSLR could reach 153 cm under high-emission scenarios, potentially flooding 65% of the lagoon's total land area and 90% of the Chioggia area [Multi-temporal relative sea level rise scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice lagoon](https://www.preventionweb.net/publication/multi-temporal-relative-sea-level-rise-scenarios-2150-venice-lagoon) [Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy) - ADS](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025EGUGA..2713617T/abstract).

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness and Failure Thresholds
The MOSE system, designed for an operational threshold of 110 cm, faces several critical constraints:
- **Wind Setup Constraint:** Research identifies a "failure point" at an RSLR of +0.40 m. Beyond this, closing the barriers enhances wind-driven water gradients inside the lagoon, causing floods in some settlements even when gates are shut [How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378383921000831).
- **Operational Frequency:** Under high-emission scenarios (RCP 8.5), the mean sea level could reach the 110 cm threshold by 2100, requiring near-permanent closure [Sea-level rise in Venice: historic and future trends (review article)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/). This would lead to the collapse of the port industry and risk turning the lagoon into a "fetid swamp" due to lack of tidal flushing [New study by Italian scientists predicts Venice will be underwater by 2150 | Euronews](https://www.euronews.com/2024/06/19/new-study-by-italian-scientists-predicts-venice-will-be-underwater-by-2150).
- **Infrastructure Vulnerability:** The MOSE inlets themselves are subsiding at rates up to 2.9 mm/year, and extreme water levels (accounting for tides and storm surges) could reach 3.47m by 2150, potentially submerging the barriers entirely [Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy) - ADS](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025EGUGA..2713617T/abstract) [Multi-temporal relative sea level rise scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice lagoon](https://www.preventionweb.net/publication/multi-temporal-relative-sea-level-rise-scenarios-2150-venice-lagoon).

While current operations provide economic benefits by avoiding real estate damage, the increasing cost of closures—estimated at  348,000 to  1.3 million per event for the port—challenges the system's long-term viability [Boon and burden: economic performance and future perspectives of the Venice flood protection system](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10113-024-02193-9).

sources.title: How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint
sources.content:
The study analyzes the effectiveness of the Mo.S.E. (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) mobile barrier system in Venice under future sea level rise (SLR) scenarios, specifically identifying the 'wind setup' as a critical failure constraint.

### Key Findings on MOSE Effectiveness & Failure Thresholds:
- **Failure Point:** The Mo.S.E. system is projected to fail in controlling flood events for all urban settlements starting from a relative sea level rise (RSLR) of **+0.40 m**. Beyond this point, the barriers cannot simultaneously protect all settlements within the lagoon due to enhanced wind setup.
- **Wind Setup Constraint:** When the lagoon inlets are closed, the action of wind on the water surface inside the lagoon is enhanced. This produces a cross-lagoon water level gradient (wind setup) that can cause flooding in certain areas even if the gates are closed. 
- **Operational Obsolescence:** While the system's expected lifetime was intended to be 100 years, near-future climate scenarios suggest it may become rapidly obsolete. Wind setup will increasingly produce 'long and repeated closures,' which conflict with port activities and lagoon ecosystem health.
- **Closure Frequency:** Climate change and RSLR will rapidly increase both the duration and frequency of barrier closures. Management will shift from occasional flood prevention to a state of near-permanent closure in high SLR scenarios.

### Impact of SLR and Flood Frequency:
- **Historical Context:** Exceptional flooding events in November and December 2019 demonstrated the vulnerability of Venice; during these periods, multiple high tides reached levels requiring intervention.
- **Comparison to IPCC Scenarios:** The study superimposed IPCC-derived RSLR scenarios on tidal records from 2000–2019. It confirms that SLR driven by climate change will exacerbate flooding frequency in the lagoon.
- **Management Issues:** The current Authority Management Protocol (AMP) is limited because it only monitors water levels at one station (Punta della Salute) and ignores internal wind setup and the propagation of tides/rain runoff after closure. 

### New Strategy (AThOS):
- The researchers propose the **Adaptive closure Threshold Operating Strategy (AThOS)**. This strategy accounts for event-specific variables including:
  - Tide propagation after closure.
  - Local rain and hydrological runoff.
  - Intra-gate infiltration (seepage between the 78 barriers).
  - Enhanced local wind setup.
- AThOS aims to identify the shortest possible closure duration to minimize economic and ecological impacts while still preventing flooding of all settlements.

### Hydrodynamic Feedbacks:
- Data from the first actual operations (Oct 2020) confirmed that once inlets are closed, wind setup becomes more clearly recognizable and measurable. 
- Intra-gate infiltration and seaward disturbance are identified as secondary factors that must be managed as sea levels rise.

sources.title: Boon and burden: economic performance and future perspectives of the Venice flood protection system
sources.content:
Venice and its lagoon face significant challenges due to Sea-Level Rise (SLR) and increasing flood frequency. The primary protection is the MoSE (Experimental Electromechanical Module), a system of 78 storm surge mobile barriers at the Lido, Malamocco, and Chioggia inlets. 

**Flood Projections and Impacts:**
- **Current Frequency:** Flooding from high tides (*acqua alta*) has steadily increased and is predicted to grow significantly. 
- **Inundation Thresholds:** Without barriers, 2% of Venice floods at 90 cm above mean sea level. At 140 cm, 59% of the city is flooded, and at 200 cm (near the 1966 record of 194 cm), over 90% is submerged.
- **IPCC Scenarios:** The study utilizes two SLR scenarios (modeled until 2100). Under the high-risk RCP 8.5 scenario, Venice is projected to be inundated by 2100 without effective intervention. 
- **Ecosystem Risk:** Frequent MoSE closures hinder water exchange and sediment accumulation on salt marshes, limiting their capacity for vertical accretion and survival against rising sea levels. This also poses a threat to lagoon water quality.

**MoSE Barrier Effectiveness:**
- **Operational Threshold:** The official closure threshold is currently set at 110 cm. While it protects against major floods, it does not prevent flooding in the lowest 2% of the city (starting at 90 cm).
- **Economic Viability:** The investment cost exceeds €6 billion, with annual operational costs in the tens of millions of euros. While economic benefits for the city (avoided damage to real estate and cultural heritage) currently outweigh these costs, the increasing frequency of closures due to SLR challenges the infrastructure's long-term viability.
- **Port Conflicts:** Closures negatively affect port activities, with direct costs to ship traffic estimated between €348,000 and €1.3 million annually. Frequent closures may lead to a 'memory effect' where ships avoid the Port of Venice.
- **Future Strategy:** The study suggests increasing the 110 cm threshold to reduce closure frequency (mitigating port and ecosystem damage), but notes this must be coordinated with other measures like sewer system consolidation to manage lower-level flooding.

sources.title: Multi-temporal relative sea level rise scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice lagoon
sources.content:
This study analyzes future scenarios of relative sea level rise (RSLR) in the Venice Lagoon up to the year 2150, integrating climatic projections from the IPCC’s Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) with geodetic measurements from GNSS and InSAR data. 

**Sea Level Rise Projections:**
- By the year 2150, RSLR could reach up to 153 cm under high-emission scenarios.
- Under the worst-case projections (SSP5-8.5), up to 65% of the land in the Venice Lagoon could be flooded.
- High-resolution Digital Surface Models (DSMs) were used to map flooding across 11 key areas, including Venice city center, industrial zones, and the airport.

**Impacts on Venice and Surrounding Areas:**
- Over 130 km² of land is projected to be threatened by 2150.
- Flooding risk is intensified by localized land subsidence, which is highly variable across the lagoon and particularly affects coastal and urban infrastructure.

**IPCC Scenarios Analyzed:**
- The study evaluates impacts based on three Shared Socioeconomic Pathways: 
  1. SSP1-2.6 (Low emissions/sustainability)
  2. SSP3-7.0 (High emissions/regional rivalry)
  3. SSP5-8.5 (Very high emissions/fossil-fuel development)

**MoSE Barrier Effectiveness:**
- The MoSE (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) flood barrier system was designed to withstand water levels up to 3 meters.
- However, the study warns that by 2150, these barriers may become insufficient and could be submerged during extreme events under high-emission scenarios.
- The findings suggest that current infrastructure adaptation may not be enough to preserve the UNESCO World Heritage Site in the face of long-term RSLR and land subsidence.

sources.title: Sea Level Rise Projection Map - Venice | Earth.Org
sources.content:
Venice is highly vulnerable to sea level rise (SLR), having experienced record floods in late 2019 that submerged 70% of the city and rose to over six feet (approx. 1.8 meters) in some areas. These floods caused €360 million in damage to public property and impacted more than 260,000 residents.

Flood Frequency Projections:
- The frequency of 'Acqua Alta' (exceptional tide peaks) has increased from fewer than 10 times per year to over 60 times per year in the last century.
- Research indicates that high emissions scenarios could lead to a global average sea level rise of 1 to 2 meters by 2100. This is expected to significantly increase the frequency of '100-year floods' (events with a 1% annual chance of occurrence).
- Extreme coastal water levels (storm surges/tides) are estimated to be 0.2m to 2.8m above the mean level, which must be added to projected SLR.

Sea Level Rise Scenarios (IPCC/Scientific Projections):
- Mild Scenario (RCP 4.5): Based on a 2°C temperature rise. Mid-range projections estimate SLR between 0.5m and 1.2m.
- Extreme Scenario (RCP 8.5): Based on a 4°C temperature rise. Pessimistic models that include ice-sheet melting mechanisms estimate SLR at 1.0m to 2.5m by 2100, and potentially 10m by 2300.
- Localized Variability: When coastal flood levels are added to SLR, Venice could face local levels up to 2.8m above Mean Higher-High Water (MHHW).

MOSE Barrier System:
- The MOSE project (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) consists of flood barriers designed to protect the city from high waters. 
- Development has been hindered by corruption, funding issues, and technical complexity since its start in 2003.
- Following the 2019 floods, the Italian government resumed work with a predicted completion date of late 2021 (as per the source text). 
- While the barriers are intended to mitigate the impacts of Acqua Alta, the text suggests that rising global sea levels (up to 2m or more) will pose an extreme challenge to Venice's long-term survival, implying a potential limit to the effectiveness of such physical defenses against the highest projected SLR scenarios.

sources.title: Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy) - ADS
sources.content:
This study (Trippanera et al., 2025) provides specific projections for Relative Sea Level Rise (RSLR) and flooding in the Venice Lagoon, integrating IPCC AR6 scenarios with local Vertical Land Movement (VLM) data.

### Subsidence and Land Stability
*   **Venice Ancient City:** Described as almost stable.
*   **MOSE Inlet Areas:** The three inlets where the MoSE (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) barriers are located are experiencing subsidence rates of up to **2.9 mm/yr** (based on GNSS and InSAR data from 1996–2023).

### Sea Level Rise and Flood Projections (IPCC AR6 Scenarios)
The study evaluated RSLR using SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5 scenarios for the years 2050, 2100, and 2150.
*   **Land at Risk (by 2150):** 
    *   **SSP1-2.6 (Low emission):** 112 km² of land exposed to flooding.
    *   **SSP5-8.5 (High emission):** 159 km² of land exposed to flooding.
*   **Extreme Water Levels (by 2150):** When accounting for the joint effects of astronomical tides, seiches, atmospheric forcing, and RSLR, temporary water levels could reach up to **3.47 m**.
*   **Spatial Flooding Impact:** 
    *   Under the extreme 3.47 m scenario, up to **65% of the total land** in the lagoon area may be flooded.
    *   In the **Chioggia area** (the lowest part), approximately **90% of the land** would be covered by water.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness
The research specifically highlights that these projected extreme water levels and RSLR values pose significant questions regarding the **durability and effectiveness** of the MoSE artificial barrier. The barrier's ability to protect the lagoon from flooding is challenged by the combination of high subsidence at the barrier's own location (up to 2.9 mm/yr) and the increasing frequency and height of extreme water events projected through 2150.

sources.title: Sea-level rise in Venice: historic and future trends (review article)
sources.content:
### Historical Sea-Level Rise Trends in Venice
*   **Total Relative Sea-Level Rise (RSLR):** Over the last ~150 years, Venice experienced an average rate of relative sea-level rise of approximately 2.5 mm/year.
*   **VLM-Corrected SLR (1872–2019):** After removing vertical land movement (subsidence) effects, the average rate of sea-level rise is estimated at 1.23 ± 0.13 mm/year.
*   **Recent Acceleration (1993–2019):** Tide-gauge data (corrected for subsidence) show an increased average rate of +2.76 ± 1.75 mm/year.
*   **Subsidence Contribution:** Vertical land movement (VLM) contributed to about half of the historical RSLR in Venice.

### Projected Relative Sea-Level Rise (RSLR) by 2100
The paper provides atmospherically corrected RSLR projections for Venice based on IPCC scenarios:
*   **RCP2.6 Scenario (Low Emissions):** Likely range of 32 cm to 62 cm of rise by 2100.
*   **RCP8.5 Scenario (High Emissions):** Likely range of 58 cm to 110 cm of rise by 2100.
*   **High-End Scenario:** A plausible but unlikely scenario linked to strong ice-sheet melting yields approximately 180 cm of RSLR by 2100.

### Key Drivers and Impacts
*   **Vertical Land Movement (VLM):** Natural subsidence is controlled by tectonics, sediment loading, and compaction. Anthropogenic subsidence (human-induced) has historically significant potential to exacerbate hazards, though specific future projections for human-induced VLM are not currently available.
*   **Atmospheric and Oceanic Processes:** Regional changes in the Adriatic and Mediterranean (linked to water mass exchange via the Strait of Gibraltar) will likely contribute significant interannual and interdecadal variability comparable to past observations.
*   **Hazard Exacerbation:** Future RSLR will directly increase the hazard posed by surges and extreme water levels. The review notes that human-induced VLM has the potential to significantly exacerbate these climatically induced changes.

### References to Flood Frequency and MOSE Effectiveness
*   The paper refers to a broader special issue ("Venice flooding: understanding, prediction capabilities, and future projections") for detailed analysis of flood phenomenology, surge prediction, and the effectiveness of the MOSE barrier system (notably citing Umgiesser et al., 2020/2021). 
*   The projections indicate that under RCP8.5, the mean sea level could reach 110 cm by 2100, which is the current operational threshold for the MOSE barrier system, suggesting the need for extremely frequent or permanent closures in high-emission scenarios.

sources.title: New study by Italian scientists predicts Venice will be underwater by 2150 | Euronews
sources.content:
A 2024 study by the National Institute of Geophysics and Volcanology (INGV) predicts that parts of Venice will be submerged by 2150. Key findings and data include:

- **Current Sea Level Rise Rates**: Tide levels in the Venetian lagoon are rising at approximately 0.5 cm per year. Localized rates vary, with 4.22 mm/year at Lido island and 5 mm/year at Malamocco, based on data from Venice’s tidal centre over the last 20 years.
- **IPCC 2100 Projections**: A 2021 IPCC report projects a mean regional sea level rise for Venice by 2100 of:
  - 28–55 cm in the most optimistic global warming scenario.
  - 63–101 cm in the most pessimistic scenario.
- **MOSE Barrier Status**: The Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico (MOSE) system of mobile barriers is currently operational, with full operation expected by 2025. The gates are raised during high tides to block water from the Adriatic Sea.
- **MOSE Long-term Effectiveness & Risks**:
  - The barriers are deemed insufficient to protect the city from the combined effects of sea level rise and land subsidence (sinking) through 2150.
  - Frequency of Use: Under high sea-level scenarios, the barriers would need to be raised so often that Venice's port industry would likely collapse.
  - Environmental Impact: Continuous closure of the lagoon would prevent tidal flushing, risking the transformation of the lagoon into a 'fetid swamp' due to lack of water exchange.
- **Subsidence**: Researcher Marco Anzidei noted that sea level increase is 'accelerated locally by subsidence,' which exacerbates coastal erosion, beach retreat, and marine flooding.

evaluation.source_discovery_rationale: The sources are exceptionally high-quality, featuring peer-reviewed journals (ScienceDirect, Springer, Copernicus) and recent 2024/2025 scientific reports that provide specific technical data on Venice's unique hydrodynamic challenges.
evaluation.extracted_content_rationale: The extracts contain highly specific data points, including precise subsidence rates at MOSE inlets (2.9 mm/yr), the specific failure threshold for wind setup (+0.40 m), and detailed inundation percentages for various water levels.
evaluation.synthesis_quality_rationale: The synthesis is comprehensive and well-structured, directly answering every part of the query. It effectively contrasts different IPCC scenarios and provides a nuanced look at the MOSE system's operational limits versus its long-term viability.

query: What are the projected sea level rise impacts on Venice specifically, including flood frequency projections and MOSE barrier effectiveness under different IPCC scenarios?
synthesis:
Venice is already experiencing a strong rise in flood hazard, driven mainly by relative sea-level rise (global sea-level rise plus local subsidence). A recent Nature study states that sea levels in Venice have risen by about **26 cm over the past century**. It also reports projected increases in **extreme total water level** of roughly **20–40 cm** for **RCP4.5 (2100)** and **RCP8.5 (2050)**, and **up to 80 cm for RCP8.5 by 2100**. [Attributing Venice Acqua Alta events to a changing climate and evaluating the efficacy of MoSE adaptation strategy](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41612-023-00513-0)

Observed flood frequency has already increased sharply. The IPCC AR6 Europe chapter says Venice’s flood record shows the evolution of **decadal frequency of floods above the safeguard level in the city centre**, and cites the first operational use of MOSE in **October 2020**. [Chapter 13: Europe | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability](https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/chapter/chapter-13/) A Scientific American summary, based on Venice flood statistics, reports that floods of at least **110 cm**—enough to inundate a significant share of the city—rose from about **once every three years in the early 20th century** to about **nine times per year in the 2010s**. [Venice Has Its Worst Flood in 53 Years](https://www.scientificamerican.com/blog/eye-of-the-storm/venice-has-its-worst-flood-in-53-years/)

For future flood frequency, one of the clearest scenario-based results comes from the Venice special-issue review in *Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences*. It concludes that inlet closures for about **3 weeks per year** are **unlikely before the 2040s** but **virtually certain before 2100 even under low emissions (RCP2.6)**, implying a major increase in the number/duration of high-water events requiring MOSE intervention. The same review says relative sea-level rise will remain the **main driver** of future increases in extreme water levels and flood potential, while changes in storminess and tides are secondary. [Venice flooding and sea level: past evolution, present issues, and future projections (introduction to the special issue)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2633/2021/)

On longer horizons and newer IPCC-style scenarios, the 2025 INGV-led study for the Venice Lagoon uses **IPCC AR6 SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5** projections combined with local vertical land movement. It finds that by **2150**, relative sea-level rise under **SSP5-8.5** may be **11–37 cm higher than the base IPCC estimate** once local vertical land motion is included, depending on the lagoon area. It also maps flood exposure for **2050, 2100, and 2150**, and reports that under **SSP5-8.5** the **maximum expected relative sea-level rise in some lagoon sectors reaches about 1.40–1.51 m by 2150**. [Multi-Temporal Relative Sea Level Rise Scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice Lagoon (Italy)](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/17/5/820) The same study’s institutional summary says that in the **worst case**, extreme high tides comparable to 1966 or 2019 could reach **3.47 m above the Punta della Salute reference by 2150** if no additional protection beyond today’s system is assumed. [CLIMATE CHANGE | Possible sea level rise scenarios highlighted in Venice](https://www.ingv.it/en/press-and-urp/Press/Press-releases/5716-climate-change-highlighted-possible-scenarios-of-sea-level-rise-in-venice)

For city impacts, current threshold relationships remain stark: San Marco begins to flood around **80 cm**, about **12% of the city** floods at around **110 cm**, and about **70%** at around **130 cm**. [The flood of Venice - Catchments.ie - Catchments.ie](https://www.catchments.ie/the-flood-of-venice/) A flood-risk study based on the **12 November 2019** event found that **50% of all structures** in Venice experienced flood depths of **0.55 m or more**, only **10%** saw less than **0.10 m**, and only **5%** were not flooded at all. [Developing a framework for the assessment of current and future flood risk in Venice, Italy](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/22/2381/2022/)

**MOSE effectiveness under different scenarios** appears to be strong in the near term but increasingly constrained as sea level rises. The NHESS flood-risk framework concludes that a **partially functioning MOSE barrier** would **significantly reduce expected average flood depth for 90% of buildings** under the lower sea-level-rise scenarios **SLR0 and SLR1**, but in **SLR2** the higher mean sea level overwhelms much of that benefit. [Developing a framework for the assessment of current and future flood risk in Venice, Italy](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/22/2381/2022/)

A more direct operational constraint comes from the NHESS special-issue review and the IPCC AR6 chapter, both drawing on Venice-system studies. They indicate that with about **50 cm** of relative sea-level rise, MOSE closures would be needed for roughly **2 months per year**, and with about **75 cm** of rise, for about **6 months per year**. [Venice flooding and sea level: past evolution, present issues, and future projections (introduction to the special issue)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2633/2021/) [Chapter 13: Europe | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability](https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/chapter/chapter-13/) That means MOSE can still physically protect Venice from many extreme tides, but its **operational viability declines** because frequent or prolonged closures disrupt navigation, lagoon water exchange, ecosystem functioning, and port activity. [How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint - ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378383921000831)

The wind-setup study adds an important caveat: standard MOSE operating rules focused on Punta della Salute do **not fully account for wind setup inside the lagoon after closure**, and closure itself can **enhance water-level differences within the basin**. That suggests that, under higher sea-level scenarios, simply raising the gates more often may not guarantee uniform protection for all lagoon settlements. [How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint - ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378383921000831)

Putting the scenario picture together:
- Under **low-emissions / low-end rise** pathways such as **RCP2.6 / SSP1-2.6**, Venice still faces worsening flood frequency and more frequent MOSE closures, but the system remains broadly useful through much of the century; however, even in this case, weeks of annual closure become likely by late century. [Venice flooding and sea level: past evolution, present issues, and future projections (introduction to the special issue)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2633/2021/) [Multi-Temporal Relative Sea Level Rise Scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice Lagoon (Italy)](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/17/5/820)
- Under **intermediate scenarios** such as **RCP4.5 / SSP3-7.0-like pathways**, extreme water levels and closure frequency rise substantially; the literature I found gives fewer Venice-specific closure counts for this exact middle case, but it clearly places it between the low-end and high-end trajectories. [Attributing Venice Acqua Alta events to a changing climate and evaluating the efficacy of MoSE adaptation strategy](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41612-023-00513-0) [Multi-Temporal Relative Sea Level Rise Scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice Lagoon (Italy)](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/17/5/820)
- Under **high-emissions scenarios** such as **RCP8.5 / SSP5-8.5**, Venice sees much larger increases in extreme water levels and flood exposure; MOSE remains effective against individual events but becomes progressively less practical because closures would be needed very often, potentially for months per year, and local hydrodynamic effects inside the lagoon become more problematic. [Attributing Venice Acqua Alta events to a changing climate and evaluating the efficacy of MoSE adaptation strategy](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41612-023-00513-0) [Venice flooding and sea level: past evolution, present issues, and future projections (introduction to the special issue)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2633/2021/) [How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint - ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378383921000831)

One important limitation in the literature is that different papers use different scenario frameworks (**RCPs** vs **SSPs**), different reference periods, and different metrics (**mean sea level**, **relative sea level**, **extreme total water level**, or **closure duration**), so values are not perfectly interchangeable. [Multi-Temporal Relative Sea Level Rise Scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice Lagoon (Italy)](https://www.mdpi.com/2072-4292/17/5/820) [Venice flooding and sea level: past evolution, present issues, and future projections (introduction to the special issue)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2633/2021) Still, they point in the same direction: **Venice can be defended in the near to medium term by MOSE, but rising sea level sharply increases flood frequency and forces a transition from “event protection” to “chronic closure management,” especially under higher-emissions IPCC scenarios.** [Chapter 13: Europe | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability](https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/chapter/chapter-13/) [Developing a framework for the assessment of current and future flood risk in Venice, Italy](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/22/2381/2022/)

I did **not** find, in the accessible sources, a single authoritative table that directly gives Venice-specific annual flood counts for each IPCC scenario in the exact format “RCP/SSP scenario → number of floods per year.” What I found instead were: observed flood-frequency trends, scenario-based changes in extreme water levels, and MOSE closure-duration thresholds. [Chapter 13: Europe | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability](https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/chapter/chapter-13/) [Venice flooding and sea level: past evolution, present issues, and future projections (introduction to the special issue)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2633/2021/)

sources.title: Attributing Venice Acqua Alta events to a changing climate and evaluating the efficacy of MoSE adaptation strategy
sources.content: Study on Venice Acqua Alta events and MoSE efficacy. States Venice sea levels have risen by approximately 26 cm over the past century. Reports projections indicating a change in extreme total water level between 20 and 40 cm for RCP4.5 (2100) and RCP8.5 (2050), and up to 80 cm for RCP8.5 (2100). Describes Acqua Alta drivers: tide plus storm surge, often associated with Genoa Low and strong winds over the Adriatic. Shows locations of tide gauges Punta della Salute and Piattaforma and MoSE barriers at the three inlets.

sources.title: Chapter 13: Europe | Climate Change 2022: Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability
sources.content: IPCC AR6 WGII Europe chapter includes Box 13.1.1 on Venice sea level rise and coastal flooding. Says figure shows evolution of relative and mean sea level in Venice and decadal frequency of floods above the safeguard level in the city centre; projected relative sea-level rise at Venetian coast under SSP scenarios; and timing when critical relative sea-level thresholds will be reached depending on scenarios and confidence levels. States construction of flood protection system started in 2003 and it was first used in October 2020. Search snippet and associated AR6 materials indicate closures for about 2 months/year at 50 cm relative sea-level rise and about 6 months/year at 75 cm rise, based on cited Venice studies.

sources.title: Venice flooding and sea level: past evolution, present issues, and future projections (introduction to the special issue)
sources.content: Review of Venice flooding and sea level. Uses RCP2.6, RCP8.5 and a high-end scenario. States stabilizing temperature does not stabilize sea level, so some relative sea-level rise is inevitable for Venice. Says relative sea-level rise will be the key factor responsible for future increase in extreme sea-level frequency and height, with meteorological and tidal changes secondary. Reports that closing the inlets for 3 weeks/year is unlikely before the 2040s but virtually certain before end of century even under low-emission RCP2.6. Cited by IPCC for timing of critical thresholds and operational implications for MOSE.

sources.title: Developing a framework for the assessment of current and future flood risk in Venice, Italy
sources.content: Flood-risk modelling study for Venice. Finds that for the storm surge of 12 November 2019, 50% of all structures in Venice experienced flood depths of 0.55 m or higher; only 10% had flood depths below 0.10 m; only 5% were not exposed. Concludes a partially functioning MOSE barrier would significantly reduce expected average flood depth for 90% of buildings under lower sea-level-rise scenarios SLR0 and SLR1. In SLR2, increased mean sea level dominates and reduces the benefit of partial closure.

sources.title: Multi-Temporal Relative Sea Level Rise Scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice Lagoon (Italy)
sources.content: INGV-led 2025 study using IPCC AR6 regional projections with SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5 plus local vertical land movement (VLM) from GNSS/InSAR. Reference year 2023 for sea-level projections. States that by 2150, under SSP8.5, relative sea-level rise may increase by 11 to 37 cm beyond IPCC estimates depending on area once local VLM is included. Provides flood maps for 2050, 2100, 2150. Reports maximum expected RSLR in some lagoon sectors by 2150 under SSP5-8.5 up to 1.40 m, 1.44 m, and 1.51 m in selected areas. Notes projected maximum water level in 2150 under SSP5-8.5 with a 1966-like storm surge can reach around 3.34 to 3.45 m depending on area.

sources.title: CLIMATE CHANGE | Possible sea level rise scenarios highlighted in Venice
sources.content: Institutional summary of the 2025 Venice Lagoon sea-level-rise study. Says the study analyzed the most up-to-date IPCC projections and geodetic data to estimate flooded surfaces for 2050, 2100, 2150. States that in the worst-case scenario, sea level in 2150 could rise up to 3.47 m above the Punta della Salute tide-gauge reference during extreme high tides similar to 1966 and 2019, in the absence of systems protecting the lagoon from sea levels higher than today.

sources.title: How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint
sources.content: Study of operational limits of MOSE. Critiques standard operational plan: focuses on Punta della Salute water level; neglects wind setup induced by local winds after inlet closure; does not provide strategy for multi-tidal-cycle closures; uses generic closure thresholds. States closure of lagoon can strongly enhance wind setup within the basin. Examines future scenarios by superimposing relative sea-level-rise values derived from IPCC reports on 2000–2019 sea-level time series. Implies protection may become less uniform across lagoon settlements under higher sea levels.

sources.title: Venice Has Its Worst Flood in 53 Years
sources.content: Reports Venice tidal flooding of at least 110 cm increased from about once every three years in early 20th century to about nine times per year in the 2010s. Notes storm tides exceeding 140 cm are classified as exceptional and flood around half the city.

sources.title: The flood of Venice - Catchments.ie
sources.content: Explains threshold impacts of high tides in Venice. Says St. Mark’s Square begins to flood around +80 cm; at +110 cm about 12% of the city floods; at +130 cm about 70% of the city floods. Also references projected increasing yearly frequency of medium-high tides (80–109 cm) and high tides (at least +110 cm).

evaluation.source_discovery_rationale: The sources are exceptionally high-quality, including the IPCC AR6, Nature, and specialized journals like NHESS, providing a perfect mix of global climate frameworks and local Venetian hydrodynamic studies.
evaluation.extracted_content_rationale: The extracts contain highly specific data points, including centimeter-scale sea level projections, specific MOSE closure duration thresholds (e.g., 3 weeks vs 6 months), and building-level impact percentages.
evaluation.synthesis_quality_rationale: The synthesis is comprehensive and expertly structured, clearly addressing the nuances of different IPCC scenarios (RCP vs SSP) and the distinction between physical barrier effectiveness and operational/ecological viability.

query: What specific Total Allowable Catch quotas has ICCAT set for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna for each year from 2022 to 2026, and what were the scientific basis and stock assessment results behind each decision?
synthesis:
The International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) transitioned the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna (EBFT) from a 'Recovery Plan' to a 'Multi-Annual Management Plan' during the 2022–2026 period. This shift reflects scientific advice that the stock no longer requires emergency measures [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf). 

### Annual Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas
- **2022:** The TAC was set at **36,000 tonnes (t)** under Recommendation 21-08, based on scientific advice from 2017 and 2020 [ICCAT Recommendation 24-05](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf) [2022 Stock Assessment Report](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2022/REPORTS/2022_EBFT_SA_ENG.pdf).
- **2023, 2024, and 2025:** The annual TAC was increased to **40,570 t** (inclusive of dead discards). This level was adopted via Recommendation 22-08 and maintained through Recommendation 24-05 [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [ICCAT Recommendation 24-05](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2024-05-e.pdf).
- **2026:** The TAC for the 2026–2028 management cycle has been set at **48,403 t**, representing a 19.3% increase over the previous period [ICCAT Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf) [Bluefin Tuna Quota Increase Approved](https://onthewater.com/news/2025/12/01/iccat-new-bluefin-and-shark-rules-2026).

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results
**2023–2025 Decisions:** These quotas were the first to be established using a formal **Management Procedure (MP)** developed through Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE) [2022 Data Preparatory Meeting](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2022/REPORTS/2022_BFT_DP_ENG.pdf). The 2022 stock assessment utilized three different models to capture scientific uncertainty, with varying results on stock status:
- **Virtual Population Analysis (VPA):** Estimated a fishing mortality ratio of 1.16, indicating overfishing was occurring [2022 Stock Assessment Report](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2022/REPORTS/2022_EBFT_SA_ENG.pdf).
- **Stock Synthesis (SS):** Estimated a ratio of 0.72, indicating no overfishing [2022 Stock Assessment Report](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2022/REPORTS/2022_EBFT_SA_ENG.pdf).
- **Age Structured Assessment Program (ASAP):** Estimated a ratio of 0.54, also indicating no overfishing [2022 Stock Assessment Report](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2022/REPORTS/2022_EBFT_SA_ENG.pdf).
Despite model discrepancies, all platforms agreed that Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB) has increased steadily since 2010 and confirmed an exceptionally strong 2003 year class [2022 Stock Assessment Report](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2022/REPORTS/2022_EBFT_SA_ENG.pdf). 

**2026 Decision:** The increase to 48,403 t for 2026 was based on the application of the current MP and the results of the 2025 workplan [ICCAT Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf). Scientific discussions leading into 2026 also focus on "rebalancing" the ratio between Eastern and Western stocks by incorporating new research on natural distribution and stock mixing through genomics and tagging studies [U.S. secures major win at 2025 ICCAT](https://www.noaa.gov/news-release/us-secures-major-win-for-bluefin-tuna-fishery-at-2025-iccat-annual-meeting).

sources.title: Recommendation 22-08: Establishing a Multi-Annual Management Plan for Bluefin Tuna in the Eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean
sources.content:
Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas (2022-2026):
- 2022: The provided document (Rec. 22-08) amends Rec. 21-08 and establishes quotas starting in 2023. It does not explicitly list the 2022 TAC, which was governed by the preceding Recommendation 21-08.
- 2023, 2024, and 2025: The TAC (inclusive of dead discards) is set at 40,570 tonnes (t) for each year.
- 2026: The TAC for 2026 and thereafter will be decided at the 2025 Commission Annual Meeting in accordance with the Management Procedure (MP).

Specific Annual Quota Allocations (2023-2025) in tonnes:
- European Union: 21,503 t
- Morocco: 3,700 t
- Japan: 3,114 t
- Tunisia: 3,000 t
- Türkiye: 2,600 t
- Libya: 2,548 t
- Algeria: 2,023 t
- Egypt: 513 t
- Norway: 368 t
- Albania: 264 t
- Iceland: 224 t
- Korea: 221 t
- Syria: 129 t
- China: 112 t
- Chinese Taipei: 101 t
- United Kingdom: 63 t
- Namibia: 50 t
- Unallocated Reserves: 37 t
- Research catches: Mauritania and Senegal may each catch up to 5 t for research annually, deducted from the unallocated reserve.
- Transfers: Chinese Taipei may transfer up to 50 t of its quota to Korea in 2023-2025. CPCs may request to carry over a maximum of 5% of their annual quota to the following year.

Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results:
- Management Shift: The Commission moved from a Recovery Plan (Rec. 17-07) to a Multi-Annual Management Plan because the Standing Committee on Research and Statistics (SCRS) advised that the stock status no longer requires emergency measures.
- Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE): The TACs for 2023 onwards are based on a completed MSE conducted by the SCRS to establish a Management Procedure (MP).
- Management Procedure (MP): The plan implements Harvest Control Rules (HCR) as established in Recommendation 22-09. This MP is used for both Western and Eastern Atlantic/Mediterranean management areas.
- Recruitment and Sustainability: Decisions consider the stock's capability to respond to consecutive years of low recruitment. Fishing capacity must remain within sustainable limits, with purse-seine vessel capacity restricted to a maximum variation of 20% compared to the 2018 baseline.
- Growth and Technical Data: The SCRS reviews growth tables (updated in 2022) and uses data from stereoscopic cameras, AI trials, and eBCD (electronic Bluefin Catch Document) systems to refine number and weight estimates. 
- Minimum Size: Set at 30 kg or 115 cm fork length, with specific derogations (8 kg or 75 cm) for certain small-scale coastal fleets (e.g., baitboats in the Adriatic or Bay of Biscay).

sources.title: Atlantic Bluefin Tuna Year in Review - Fall 2025 HMS Advisory Panel Presentation
sources.content:
According to the presentation, the International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) has established the following for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean (EBFT) bluefin tuna:

### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas
*   **2023-2025:** The annual TAC for EBFT is set at **40,570 mt** per year (referenced as Rec. 24-05).
*   **2026-2028:** ICCAT's scientific body is anticipated to provide Management Procedure (MP)-based TAC advice for this management cycle in Fall 2025. The specific quota for 2026 has not yet been finalized but will be based on the MP.
*   **2022:** The document notes that Management Procedures (establishing TACs for 2023-2025) were adopted in 2022, but it does not explicitly list the specific EBFT TAC value for the year 2022 itself, as the new MP-based TACs officially started in 2023.

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results
*   **Management Procedure (MP):** In 2022, ICCAT adopted a Management Procedure (Rec. 22-09, updated in Rec. 23-07) to establish TACs for both management areas (Western and Eastern). This MP was developed over many years and provides a pre-agreed framework for setting catch limits based on scientific data.
*   **Stock Status (as of 2021):** The scientific basis for current management includes the finding that **no overfishing is occurring** for the EBFT stock. The "overfished" status was not assessed by ICCAT as of the 2021 assessment.
*   **Upcoming Assessments:** A new stock assessment is currently anticipated to take place in **2026**.
*   **Decision Process:** TAC and quota recommendations are endorsed by members of ICCAT’s Panel 2 (temperate tunas) and adopted by ICCAT via consensus.

### General Context
*   **Management Parties:** There are 17 parties involved in the management of EBFT, including the European Union.
*   **Quota Management:** ICCAT includes provisions to address quota under- and overharvest. For example, under Rec. 22-10 (referenced for WBFT but illustrative of the process), underharvest may be carried forward (up to 10% of base quota), while overharvest results in a 100% reduction of the following year's quota (or 125% for consecutive years).

sources.title: ICCAT Recommendation 24-05: Multi-annual Management Plan for Bluefin Tuna in the Eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean
sources.content:
### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas (2023–2026)
- **2023–2025:** The TAC for each year from 2023 to 2025 is set at **40,570 tonnes (t)**, inclusive of dead discards.
- **2026:** The TAC for 2026 and thereafter will be decided at the 2025 Commission Annual Meeting in accordance with the Management Procedure (MP).
- **2022:** While this specific document (Rec. 24-05) focuses on the 2023–2025 period, it notes that the Commission decided on the Management Procedure at its 2022 Annual Meeting to establish TACs for 2023 and thereafter. (Note: The 2022 TAC of 36,000 t was established under the preceding Recommendation 21-08, which this plan amends).

### Annual Quota Allocations for 2023–2025 (Selected CPCs):
- **European Union:** 21,503 t
- **Morocco:** 3,700 t
- **Japan:** 3,114 t
- **Tunisia:** 3,000 t
- **Türkiye:** 2,600 t
- **Libya:** 2,548 t
- **Algeria:** 2,023 t
- **Egypt:** 513 t
- **Norway:** 368 t
- **Albania:** 264 t
- **Iceland:** 224 t (Iceland may transfer up to 200 t to the EU)
- **Korea:** 221 t (May receive up to 50 t from Chinese Taipei)
- **Syria:** 129 t
- **China:** 112 t
- **Chinese Taipei:** 101 t
- **United Kingdom:** 63 t
- **Namibia:** 50 t
- **Unallocated Reserves:** 37 t (Mauritania and Senegal may each catch up to 5 t for research, deducted from this reserve).

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results
- **Transition to Management Plan:** The SCRS (Standing Committee on Research and Statistics) advised moving from the previous 'Recovery Plan' to a 'Management Plan' because the current status of the stock no longer appears to require emergency measures. 
- **Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE):** The TACs for 2023–2025 were established based on a completed MSE used to develop a formal Management Procedure (MP).
- **Harvest Control Rules (HCR):** The MP includes specific Harvest Control Rules adopted by the Commission at the 2022 Annual Meeting to ensure sustainable exploitation.
- **Stock Resilience:** The decision-making process took into account the stock's capability to respond to several consecutive years of low recruitment.
- **Capacity Management:** The plan requires fishing capacity to remain within sustainable limits, commensurate with allocated quotas, based on catch rates proposed by the SCRS and adopted in 2009 (to be reviewed with each new stock assessment).

### Technical and Management Measures
- **Minimum Size:** 30 kg or 115 cm fork length (with specific derogations for 8 kg/75 cm in certain small-scale coastal and artisanal fisheries).
- **Purse Seine Season:** Generally 26 May to 1 July (with regional variations, such as 15 May in the Eastern Mediterranean or 25 June–15 November in Norwegian/Icelandic waters).
- **By-catch:** Limited to 20% of total catches on board; all dead by-catch must be deducted from the CPC quota.

sources.title: ICCAT Press Release - 29th Regular Meeting of the Commission (November 2025)
sources.content:
The International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) met in Seville, Spain, on 24 November 2025, to establish conservation and management measures, including new Total Allowable Catches (TACs) for bluefin tuna.

### Bluefin Tuna TAC Quotas (2026-2028)
- **Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna:** The TAC for the 2026-2028 period was set at **48,403 t**. This represents a **19.3% increase** over the previous TAC levels (which, based on the increase, were approximately 40,570 t for 2023-2025).
- **Western Atlantic Bluefin Tuna:** The TAC for the 2026-2028 period was set at **3,081.6 t**, representing a **13% increase** over the previous levels (approximately 2,727 t).

### Scientific Basis and Assessment Results
- **Management Procedures:** The decisions to set the 2026-2028 TACs for both western and eastern bluefin tuna were based on the "current management procedure" and an evaluation of the results from the 2025 workplan.
- **Assessment Workplan:** While full scientific stock assessments in 2025 were specifically highlighted for bigeye tuna (*Thunnus obesus*), white marlin (*Kajikia albida*), and Atlantic shortfin mako shark (*Isurus oxyrinchus*), the bluefin tuna quotas were adjusted according to the established multi-annual management plan and compliance reviews.
- **Regulatory Framework:** The Commission adopted Recommendation PA2_636C_REV_1, which amends Recommendation 24-05, establishing the multi-annual management plan for eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna. For the western stock, the Commission amended Recommendation 22-10.

### Other Related Measures
- **Skipjack Tuna:** A management procedure was implemented for western Atlantic skipjack to set a constant TAC of 30,844 t for 2026-2028.
- **Shark Conservation:** A maximum mortality objective of 1,000 t was set for southern Atlantic shortfin mako. Retention of basking sharks and great white sharks is now prohibited.
- **Canary Islands Pilot Project:** A pilot project (PA1_502A/PA2_609A) was adopted to assess fishing and farming conditions for bluefin and yellowfin tuna in FAO zone 34.1.

sources.title: Report of the 2022 ICCAT Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment Meeting
sources.content:
The 2022 stock assessment for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna (BFT-E) utilized three modeling platforms: Virtual Population Analysis (VPA), Stock Synthesis (SS), and the Age-Structured Assessment Programme (ASAP). 

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results:
1. **Stock Trends:** All three models showed similar trends in Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB), with a strong increase observed since the late 2000s. Recruitment estimates in recent years also indicated a clear increase, though the magnitude remains uncertain due to retrospective analysis issues.
2. **Stock Status (Fcurrent / F0.1):** The models provided different estimates of stock status relative to the F0.1 reference level:
   - **VPA (Run 385):** Reported an Fcurrent/F0.1 ratio of 1.16, indicating that overfishing was occurring. SSB decreased from the 1970s to the 1990s and increased from 2010.
   - **Stock Synthesis (Run 16 reweight):** Reported an Fcurrent/F0.1 ratio of 0.72, indicating overfishing was NOT occurring. The terminal harvest rate in 2020 was estimated at approximately 7% of total biomass.
   - **ASAP:** Reported an Fcurrent/F0.1 ratio of 0.542, indicating overfishing was NOT occurring.
3. **Key Model Changes:** 
   - Revised Catch-at-Size (CAS) for the 'inflated catch' period (1998–2007) based on the assumption that unreported catch was preferentially medium and large fish.
   - In Stock Synthesis, growth parameters were fixed with Linf = 271 cm using a Richards growth model.
   - Selectivity assumptions were adjusted, showing a substantial change in the late 1990s (from selecting young ages to older ages) due to increased regulation and monitoring.

### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas:
This July 2022 report served as the scientific basis for management decisions but did not finalize the 2023–2026 quotas. It notes: "Preliminary projection results were reviewed, however the Group decided to discuss further the management advice at the Species Group meeting in September 2022." 

Contextual data for the query period (based on subsequent ICCAT Recommendations 21-08 and 22-08, which utilized this assessment):
- **2022 TAC:** 36,000 tonnes (established prior to this assessment by Rec. 21-08).
- **2023, 2024, and 2025 TAC:** 40,570 tonnes (set following this assessment's advice by Rec. 22-08).
- **2026 TAC:** Not explicitly finalized in this 2022 technical report; typically set during the following management cycle or as a rollover.

sources.title: Report of the 2022 Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Data Preparatory Meeting (Including BFT MSE)
sources.content:
### Scientific Basis for TAC Quotas (2022-2026)
The scientific basis for setting Total Allowable Catch (TAC) for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean (E-BFT) bluefin tuna for the 2022-2026 period shifted from traditional stock assessments to a **Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE)** framework. This involves testing **Candidate Management Procedures (CMPs)** against a reference grid of **Operating Models (OMs)** that capture uncertainties in biology and fishery dynamics.

*   **Management Procedures (MPs):** The group evaluated eight CMPs (e.g., AI, EA, BR, LW, PW, TC, FZ) designed to set TACs based on abundance indices (such as Japanese longline and western Mediterranean larval indices) rather than annual model-based biomass estimates.
*   **Reference Points:** A **Biomass Limit Reference Point (BLIM)** was set at **40% of dynamic SSBMSY** (0.4 SSBMSY). Management objectives aim for a probability of less than 15% (and ideally 5%) of the stock falling below this level between years 11 and 30 of the management period.
*   **Tuning Targets:** CMPs were tuned to achieve target depletion levels (SSB relative to dynamic SSBMSY) by year 30 (Br30). The primary tuning targets discussed were **1.25 for West BFT** and **1.50 for East BFT**.
*   **TAC Change Constraints:** To ensure industry stability, Panel 2 requested evaluating constraints such as a maximum **20% increase** and **30% decrease** (+20/-30%) in TAC per management cycle. A transition phase of **+20%/-10%** for the first two management cycles (first four years) was also considered to phase in any large reductions.

### Stock Assessment Results and Observations
*   **Stock Status:** Indices of abundance indicate the stock is **"rebuilding to levels to which had not been seen recently"** and reflect "recent generally high indices."
*   **Recruitment:** The 2022 conditioning of OMs identified a **"large positive recruitment anomaly"** in the East area, driven by a recent increase in the western Mediterranean larval index.
*   **OM Reference Grid:** OMs represent three recruitment scenarios: **R1 (high)**, **R2 (low)**, and **R3 (intermediate)**. Tuning including R3 levels was found to not significantly bias performance compared to the traditional R1/R2 dichotomy.

### Specific TAC Quotas (2022-2026)
*   **2022 TAC:** The TAC for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna in 2022 was previously set at **36,000 tonnes** (as established by Rec. 21-08). The report notes that the 2022 West BFT TAC was updated to a higher level than previously used in model conditioning.
*   **2023–2025 TAC:** While this preparatory report focuses on the development of the MP, the subsequent Commission meeting (based on these scientific results) adopted **Recommendation 22-08**, which set the E-BFT TAC at **40,570 tonnes annually for 2023, 2024, and 2025** based on the first application of the selected Management Procedure.
*   **2026 TAC:** Quotas beyond 2025 are intended to be determined by the next scheduled application of the Management Procedure (every 2 or 3 years).

sources.title: 2022 ICCAT Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna Stock Assessment Report
sources.content:
### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas (2022-2026)
Based on ICCAT Recommendations and the scientific advice provided in the 2022 assessment:
- **2022:** 36,000 t (Set by Recommendation 21-08, following the 2017 and 2020 stock advice).
- **2023-2025:** 40,570 t annually (Adopted via Recommendation 22-09). This quota was determined by the first implementation of a Management Procedure (MP), which was evaluated and verified using the 2022 stock assessment results.
- **2026:** To be determined (The 2022 MP typically sets quotas in 3-year cycles; the 40,570 t quota applies through 2025, with the next update expected for the 2026-2028 period).

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results (2022 Assessment)
The 2022 assessment was the first to successfully use three different modeling platforms—Virtual Population Analysis (VPA), Stock Synthesis (SS), and Age Structured Assessment Program (ASAP)—to address scientific uncertainties.

#### 1. Stock Status and Overfishing Results
- **VPA Model:** Estimated a current fishing mortality ratio ($F_{CURRENT}/F_{0.1}$) of **1.16**, indicating that **overfishing was occurring**. This model was more pessimistic, showing lower biomass levels and a slower rate of recovery.
- **Stock Synthesis (SS) Model:** Estimated an exploitation rate ratio ($F_{CURRENT}/F_{0.1}$) of **0.72**, indicating **no overfishing**. It estimated a terminal year (2020) harvest rate of approximately 7% of total biomass.
- **ASAP Model:** Estimated a ratio of **0.54**, also indicating **no overfishing** and suggesting the greatest increase in stock biomass.

#### 2. Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB) and Recruitment Trends
- **SSB Trends:** All three models agreed that SSB decreased from the 1970s until 2007 (when the Recovery Plan, Rec. 07-04, was implemented). Since 2010, there has been a sharp, steady increase in SSB across all platforms, though the magnitude varies.
- **Recruitment:** Two distinct periods were identified: lower recruitment before 1990 and higher recruitment thereafter. All models confirmed a exceptionally strong **2003 year class**. Recent recruitment (since 2011) shows an increasing trend but carries high uncertainty due to a lack of recent data signals in the models.
- **Fishing Mortality (F):** F for ages 2-5 dropped significantly in 2007. Adult F (age 10+) also decreased drastically following the 2007 Recovery Plan, though recent increases in TAC are reflected in rising fishing mortality.

#### 3. Key Assumptions and Uncertainties
- **Inflated Catch Period (1998-2007):** Significant uncertainty remains regarding the true value of unreported catches during this period. The ASAP model assumed a 20% CV on catch data, while VPA and SS used near-exact fits to 'inflated' catch estimates.
- **Growth and Maturity:** The Group fixed the asymptotic length ($L_{INF}$) at 271 cm for the Stock Synthesis model, using a Richards growth function and a Beverton-Holt stock-recruitment relationship (steepness 0.9, sigmaR 0.6).
- **MP Decision:** Because the three models produced diverging results regarding overfishing (VPA vs. others), ICCAT moved to a Management Procedure (MP) framework to manage the stock under uncertainty, leading to the 40,570 t TAC for 2023-2025.

sources.title: 2022 ASAP Stock Assessment of the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna
sources.content:
The provided document details the 2022 stock assessment for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna (BFT-E), which serves as the scientific basis for ICCAT's Total Allowable Catch (TAC) decisions for the subsequent period. 

### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas
- **2022 TAC:** 36,000 mt (confirmed as the quota used in projections and the established limit for that year).
- **2023–2025 Projections:** The scientific paper evaluates the impact of maintaining the 'current TAC' (36,000 mt) versus other scenarios. It notes that at 36,000 mt, a 'modest increase' in the stock was projected through 2025. (Note: While this paper provides the basis, the formal ICCAT Recommendation 22-08 eventually set the TAC at **40,570 mt** for each year from 2023 to 2025 based on these results).

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results (2022 Assessment)
- **Model Used:** The Age Structured Assessment Program (ASAP), applying both single-fleet and multi-fleet configurations to data from 1968–2020.
- **Stock Status:** The assessment concluded that the stock recovered significantly over the last decade. 
- **Fishing Mortality (F):** The estimate for 2020 fishing mortality was found to be 'much less than F0.1' (a proxy for MSY). Fishing mortality had increased from the 1980s to 2007 but declined sharply thereafter due to management regulations.
- **Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB):** The 2020 spawning biomass estimate was 'much greater than SSBF0.1'. SSB declined from the late 1970s to 2007 but has since rapidly increased.
- **Recruitment:** Estimates were above average since 1987, with particularly strong recruitment cohorts observed in the early 1990s and between 2013–2017.
- **Selectivity Changes:** A substantial shift in fishery selectivity occurred in the late 1990s. Before 1999, there was full selection of young fish (ages 2-3); since 1999, selectivity shifted toward older fish (ages 9-12), with lower mortality on juveniles (ages 1-8).
- **Maturity and Growth:** Maturity was assumed at 25% at age-3, 50% at age-4, and 100% at ages 5+. Natural mortality (M) was age-specific, decreasing from 0.38 (age-1) to 0.10 (ages 10+).
- **Abundance Indices:** The assessment utilized 10 indices, including Moroccan-Spanish and Moroccan-Portuguese traps, Japanese longline (Northeast Atlantic and Mediterranean), Spanish baitboat, French aerial surveys, and Western Mediterranean larval surveys.

### Projection Results
Short-term projections (2021–2025) indicated:
- At the 36,000 mt TAC, the stock would continue to increase modestly.
- Fishing at F0.1 would allow for a 'substantial increase' in catch but would result in a 'modest stock decrease'.
- The stock-recruit relationship suggests the stock has sufficient productivity to replace itself if fished at candidate reference points.

sources.title: U.S. secures major win for bluefin tuna fishery at 2025 ICCAT annual meeting | National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration
sources.content:
At the 2025 International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) annual meeting (November 17–24, 2025, in Seville, Spain), the U.S. delegation secured a significant increase in the Western Atlantic bluefin tuna quota. 

### Quotas and Allocation Decisions:
- **Western Atlantic Bluefin Tuna (U.S. Allocation):** Negotiators secured an additional 231 metric tons, a 17% increase in the baseline quota and allowances for U.S. fishermen. This increase in the total allowable catch (TAC) is set to begin in 2026. 
- **North Atlantic Swordfish:** The United States maintained its historic quota of 3,907 metric tons. The Commission adopted the final component of a management procedure for this stock.
- **Western Skipjack Tuna:** A management procedure was adopted to maintain stability and sustainability for this species.
- **Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna:** While the article does not list the specific annual TAC numbers for the Eastern stock for 2022–2026, it notes that the 2025 meeting secured a commitment from all 55 ICCAT Parties to discuss the natural distribution and mixing of the Mediterranean and Gulf of Mexico stocks at a meeting in March 2026. This is intended to "rebalance the outdated ratio" between the Eastern and Western TACs.

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results:
- **Advanced Research:** Decisions are increasingly based on the incorporation of advanced genomics research and tagging studies into management strategies. 
- **Stock Mixing:** A key scientific focus is the "natural distribution and mixing" of the two primary stocks (Mediterranean/Eastern and Gulf of Mexico/Western). New research aims to provide a more accurate understanding of how these populations interact, which is expected to justify the rebalancing of quotas.
- **Management Procedures:** The Commission is moving toward formal "management procedures" for several stocks (skipjack and swordfish) to ensure long-term stability and sustainability based on scientific models.

### Future Actions and Scientific Discussions:
- **March 2026 Meeting:** A dedicated meeting will be held to incorporate genomics and tagging data specifically to address the Eastern/Western stock ratio.
- **Unresolved Issues:** No agreement was reached on shark conservation (specifically requiring fins to be naturally attached) or updates to rebuilding plans for blue and white marlin and yellowfin tuna management.

query: What specific Total Allowable Catch quotas has ICCAT set for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna for each year from 2022 to 2026, and what were the scientific basis and stock assessment results behind each decision?
synthesis:
Here are the **ICCAT Total Allowable Catches (TACs) for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna (BFT-E)** for each year **2022–2026**, along with the **scientific basis and stock-assessment context used for each decision**.

## TACs by year

- **2022:** **36,000 t**. ICCAT Rec. 21-08 states that the TAC for 2022, inclusive of dead discards, was set at **36,000 t** in accordance with SCRS advice. [21-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2021-08-e.pdf)
- **2023:** **40,570 t**. ICCAT Rec. 22-08 set the TAC for **2023–2025** at **40,570 t** under the adopted management procedure (MP). [22-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)
- **2024:** **40,570 t**. The same 2023–2025 multi-year TAC remained in force. [22-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [ICCAT REPORT 2024-2025 (I)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/BienRep/REP_EN_24-25-I-1.pdf)
- **2025:** **40,570 t**. ICCAT’s management plan and 2024 report confirm the TAC for 2025 remained **40,570 t**. [22-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [ICCAT REPORT 2024-2025 (I)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/BienRep/REP_EN_24-25-I-1.pdf)
- **2026:** **48,403 t**. ICCAT’s 2025 annual-meeting press release states that the Commission set the eastern bluefin TAC for **2026–2028** at **48,403 t**, based on the current management procedure. [Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

## Year-by-year scientific basis and stock-assessment results

### 2022 TAC = 36,000 t
The 2022 TAC was based on **SCRS advice first given in 2020 and reaffirmed in 2021**. Rec. 21-08 explicitly says SCRS recommended in its **2020 advice** that TAC be **maintained at 36,000 t for 2022**, to be reviewed in 2021 using updated abundance indicators, and that SCRS in **2021 confirmed** that examining the updated eastern abundance indicators and projections from the **2017 assessment** provided **no evidence to alter** the 36,000 t advice. [21-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2021-08-e.pdf)

The underlying 2021 SCRS advice says the Group examined **updated eastern abundance indicators** to evaluate whether the recommended TAC for 2022 should change. It concluded that inspection of the **updated biomass indicators** and the **projections of the 2017 assessment** did **not provide any evidence to alter the current management advice**, so **no change** from **36,000 t** was recommended for 2022. [REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE ON RESEARCH AND STATISTICS (SCRS)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2021/REPORTS/2021_SCRS_ENG.pdf)

So the **scientific basis for 2022 was not a new benchmark assessment**, but rather an **indicator-based review** of whether new abundance information was consistent with the 2017 assessment projections. The answer from SCRS was yes: the indicators did not justify changing the TAC. [REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE ON RESEARCH AND STATISTICS (SCRS)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2021/REPORTS/2021_SCRS_ENG.pdf) [21-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2021-08-e.pdf)

### 2023 TAC = 40,570 t
The 2023 TAC marked a major shift: ICCAT moved from ad hoc annual/periodic advice to a **formal Management Procedure (MP)**. Rec. 22-08 states that the TACs for **2023–2025** were set at **40,570 t** “**in accordance with the MP**.” [22-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)

The Commission’s MP framework was adopted in 2022 and then amended in 2023 through Rec. 23-07. That recommendation says the MP is to be used to calculate TACs for both western and eastern management areas, and that the Commission adopts TACs based on the MP outcome unless SCRS identifies **exceptional circumstances (ECs)**. It also states the MP’s performance measures were evaluated using **48 simulations/replicates for each of 48 operating models over a 30-year projection**. [23-07 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2023-07-e.pdf)

For the eastern stock specifically, ICCAT’s 2022–2023 report says the **three-year TACs from the adopted management procedure are, by design, intended to ensure a high probability of maintaining stock status above BMSY and avoiding overfishing**. The same report gives the stock summary used around that decision: **current reported catch (2022) 35,102 t**, **relative fishing mortality Fcurrent/F0.1 = 0.81 (0.48–1.62)**, and **stock status (2020): overfishing = No**. It also notes that **biomass reference points were not estimated since the 2017 assessment due to uncertainty in recruitment potential**. [ICCAT REPORT 2022-2023 (II)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/BienRep/REP_EN_22-23-II-2.pdf)

In other words, the **scientific basis for the 2023 TAC** was the newly adopted **MSE-derived management procedure**, informed by the **2022 eastern stock assessment** and associated operating models, rather than a single-point benchmark estimate such as a new MSY catch figure. The management framework’s explicit purpose was to maintain stock status above BMSY with high probability while avoiding overfishing. [23-07 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2023-07-e.pdf) [ICCAT REPORT 2022-2023 (II)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/BienRep/REP_EN_22-23-II-2.pdf)

### 2024 TAC = 40,570 t
The TAC for 2024 remained **40,570 t** because the **2023–2025 multi-year TAC** established under Rec. 22-08 was still in force. [22-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)

The scientific basis in 2024 was therefore not a fresh TAC-setting exercise, but an **annual check under the MP/EC framework**. Rec. 23-07 requires SCRS to assess **exceptional circumstances annually** and says the Commission should keep TACs based on the MP unless such circumstances are found. [23-07 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2023-07-e.pdf)

ICCAT’s 2024 SCRS report states that the management plan established in Rec. 22-08, based on the MP, sets a TAC of **40,570 t for 2023 to 2025**, and that according to the EC protocol in Rec. 23-07, **no EC exists that would warrant deviating from the TAC calculated under the MP for 2025**. The same report summarizes stock information as **current reported catch (2023) 39,247 t**, **Fcurrent/F0.1 = 0.81 (0.48–1.62)**, and **stock status (2020): overfishing = No**. [REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2024/Reports/2024_SCRS_ENG.pdf)

Because 2024 sat inside the fixed 2023–2025 TAC block, the scientific result relevant to that year was essentially: **the eastern stock still did not trigger exceptional-circumstances concerns significant enough to depart from the MP-derived TAC path**. [REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2024/Reports/2024_SCRS_ENG.pdf) [23-07 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2023-07-e.pdf)

### 2025 TAC = 40,570 t
The TAC for 2025 also remained **40,570 t** under the same multi-year measure for 2023–2025. [22-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [ICCAT REPORT 2024-2025 (I)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/BienRep/REP_EN_24-25-I-1.pdf)

The scientific basis was the same MP-plus-EC structure. The 2024 SCRS report explicitly says that **no exceptional circumstance exists that would warrant deviating from the TAC calculated under the MP for 2025**. [REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2024/Reports/2024_SCRS_ENG.pdf)

So for **2025**, the relevant scientific conclusion was not a new stock assessment with a newly calculated TAC, but an **annual exceptional-circumstances review** concluding that the MP-derived TAC should stand. The stock summary available to managers remained **overfishing: No**, with **Fcurrent/F0.1 = 0.81 (0.48–1.62)** and biomass reference points still not estimated because of recruitment uncertainty. [REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2024/Reports/2024_SCRS_ENG.pdf)

### 2026 TAC = 48,403 t
For 2026, ICCAT’s 2025 annual-meeting press release says the Commission agreed on new TACs for western and eastern bluefin tuna, **based on the current management procedure**, and that the eastern TAC for **2026–2028** was set at **48,403 t**, representing a **19.3% increase**. [Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

As of the sources found here, I did **not** find the full 2025 recommendation text or a 2025 SCRS eastern-bluefin stock summary explicitly laying out the detailed numerical stock-status diagnostics used for the 2026 eastern TAC in the same way the 2023–2025 decision is documented. What the official ICCAT source clearly states is that the 2026–2028 TAC was set **using the current MP**, not a newly reported 2025 benchmark assessment for the eastern stock. The same press release notes that in 2025 the full scientific stock assessments carried out were for **bigeye tuna, white marlin, and Atlantic shortfin mako shark**, not eastern bluefin tuna. [Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

That implies the **scientific basis for the 2026 eastern bluefin TAC** was again the **application of the adopted MP**, presumably after the scheduled update cycle and annual EC checks, rather than a new full eastern-stock benchmark assessment in 2025. This is an inference from ICCAT’s MP framework and 2025 press release, not a directly quoted statement of the exact eastern-stock diagnostic table for 2026. [23-07 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2023-07-e.pdf) [Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

## Concise table

| Year | ICCAT TAC for Eastern Atlantic & Mediterranean bluefin tuna | Immediate decision basis | Scientific basis / stock-assessment result cited by ICCAT |
|---|---:|---|---|
| 2022 | 36,000 t | Rec. 21-08 | 2020 SCRS advice, reaffirmed by 2021 review of updated abundance indicators and 2017-assessment projections; no evidence to change TAC. [21-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2021-08-e.pdf) [REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE ON RESEARCH AND STATISTICS (SCRS)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2021/REPORTS/2021_SCRS_ENG.pdf) |
| 2023 | 40,570 t | Rec. 22-08 | First TAC under adopted MP; designed to maintain stock above BMSY with high probability and avoid overfishing. Stock summary used by ICCAT: catch 35,102 t (2022), Fcurrent/F0.1 0.81 (0.48–1.62), overfishing: No; biomass reference points not estimated due to recruitment uncertainty. [22-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [ICCAT REPORT 2022-2023 (II)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/BienRep/REP_EN_22-23-II-2.pdf) [23-07 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2023-07-e.pdf) |
| 2024 | 40,570 t | Same 2023–2025 TAC block | Annual EC review under MP framework; no EC justifying deviation. Stock summary reported: catch 39,247 t (2023), Fcurrent/F0.1 0.81 (0.48–1.62), overfishing: No. [22-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2024/Reports/2024_SCRS_ENG.pdf) [23-07 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2023-07-e.pdf) |
| 2025 | 40,570 t | Same 2023–2025 TAC block | 2024 SCRS said no exceptional circumstances existed to deviate from MP TAC for 2025; stock summary remained overfishing: No, Fcurrent/F0.1 0.81 (0.48–1.62). [REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2024/Reports/2024_SCRS_ENG.pdf) [22-08 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) |
| 2026 | 48,403 t | 2025 ICCAT annual meeting | ICCAT says new 2026–2028 TAC was set based on the current MP; press release reports a 19.3% increase. No detailed 2025 eastern-stock assessment summary for this TAC was found in the sources retrieved here. [Press Release 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf) [23-07 BFT](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2023-07-e.pdf) |

## Important caveat
