A key limitation in ICCAT’s published eastern-bluefin summaries is that **biomass reference points were not estimated after the 2017 assessment because of uncertainty in recruitment potential**, so ICCAT’s published summaries for this stock emphasize **overfishing status and MP performance** more than a conventional annual “overfished/not overfished” biomass diagnosis. [ICCAT REPORT 2022-2023 (II)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/BienRep/REP_EN_22-23-II-2.pdf) [REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2024/Reports/2024_SCRS_ENG.pdf)

If you want, I can do a second pass and extract the **exact stock-assessment wording for 2022, 2023, 2024, 2025, and 2026 from the underlying SCRS assessment and MSE documents**, including the **operating-model assumptions, performance metrics, and exceptional-circumstances indicators** in more technical detail.

sources.title: 21-08 BFT — Recommendation by ICCAT amending the Recommendation 19-04 amending Recommendation 18-02 establishing a multi-annual management plan for bluefin tuna in the eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean
sources.content: Rec. 21-08. Preamble states: SCRS recommended in its 2020 advice that a TAC be maintained at 36,000 t for 2022 and this figure be reviewed in 2021 based on updates of the abundance indicator. It also states SCRS confirmed in its 2021 advice that examination of updated eastern abundance indicators and projections of the 2017 assessment did not provide any evidence to alter the current TAC advice of 36,000 t for 2022. Paragraph 5 sets the total allowable catches (TACs), inclusive of dead discards, for 2022 at 36,000 t, in accordance with the SCRS advice. It states TACs for 2023 and thereafter shall be decided at the 2022 Commission annual meeting in accordance with a management procedure (MP) or based on new SCRS advice in 2022 if the MP is not available yet. Allocation table for 2022 totals 36,000 t, with subtotal 35,885 and unallocated reserves 115.

sources.title: REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE ON RESEARCH AND STATISTICS (SCRS), 2021
sources.content: Section 21.24 addresses the annual advice on the TAC for eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna. Background cites that TACs for 2021 and 2022 were set at 36,000 t and that the 2022 TAC should be reviewed in 2021 based on new SCRS advice. SCRS states it examined updated eastern abundance indicators to evaluate whether it was necessary to change the current TAC advice of 36,000 t recommended for 2022. The inspection of the updated biomass indicators and the projections of the 2017 assessment did not provide any evidence to alter the current management advice. Therefore, no change in the current TAC advice of 36,000 t is recommended for 2022. The report references updated indices compared with the 80% prediction intervals from the 2017 VPA projected forward with observed catches and 6-year average recruitment. It notes the methodology used to produce the Western Mediterranean Larval Index had been substantially revised since the 2017 stock assessment.

sources.title: 22-08 BFT — Recommendation by ICCAT amending the Recommendation 21-08 establishing a multi-annual management plan for bluefin tuna in the eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean
sources.content: Paragraph 4 sets the total allowable catches (TACs), inclusive of dead discards, for 2023 to 2025 at 40,570 t, in accordance with the management procedure (MP). It states TACs for 2026 and thereafter shall be decided at the 2025 Commission Annual Meeting in accordance with the MP. Allocation table for 2023-2025 totals 40,570 t, with subtotal 40,533 and unallocated reserves 37. Allocations include EU 21,503 t, Japan 3,114 t, Morocco 3,700 t, Tunisia 3,000 t, Türkiye 2,600 t, Libya 2,548 t, Algeria 2,023 t, etc. The measure operationalizes the shift from prior advice-based TACs to MP-based TACs for the eastern stock.

sources.title: 23-07 BFT — Recommendation by ICCAT amending Recommendation 22-09 establishing a management procedure for Atlantic bluefin tuna to be used for both the western Atlantic and eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean management areas
sources.content: Rec. 23-07 establishes the framework for applying the management procedure (MP) to both western and eastern Atlantic bluefin tuna management areas. It states the MP shall be used to calculate TACs for both management areas. The Commission shall adopt TACs based on the outcome of the MP unless the SCRS identifies exceptional circumstances (ECs) requiring alternative management actions. SCRS shall assess the occurrence of ECs annually and the Commission shall act in accordance with the EC protocol in Annex 4. Annex 1 says performance measures are calculated based on 48 simulations/replicates for each of the 48 operating models of a 30-year projection under a candidate management procedure. Annex 2 describes the BR MP as empirical and based on abundance indices standardized and aggregated for East or West. Annex 4 details EC principles, annual checks, and actions if ECs are found, including alternative management options, whether the MP needs adjustment, and whether an interim stock assessment or other TAC-advice method is needed.

sources.title: ICCAT REPORT 2022-2023 (II)
sources.content: Contains eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna summary and management recommendation. States: the TAC recommendation for 2022 is unlikely to have resulted in overfishing relative to F0.1. States that the three-year TACs from the adopted management procedure are, by design, intended to ensure a high probability of maintaining stock status above BMSY and avoiding overfishing. In BFT-E-6 management recommendations, the management plan established in Rec. 22-08 and based on the MP sets a TAC for BFT-E of 40,570 t for 2023 to 2025. According to the proposed EC provisions reviewed in 2023, no EC exists that would warrant deviating from the TAC advice under the MP. Summary table: current reported catch (2022) 35,102 t as of September 2023; relative fishing mortality Fcurrent/F0.1 (2020) = 0.81 with approximate 95% CI 0.48-1.62; stock status (2020): overfishing no; TAC 2023-2025 40,570 t. Footnotes state biomass reference points to determine stock status were not estimated since the 2017 assessment due to uncertainty in recruitment potential.

sources.title: REPORT OF THE STANDING COMMITTEE (SCRS), 2024
sources.content: Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna section states the TAC for 2023 to 2025 is set at 40,570 t. The Committee noted reported catches in 2023 are in line with the TACs, though it had been informed of unquantified illegal catches. In management recommendations, the report states the management plan established in Rec. 22-08 and based on the MP sets a TAC for BFT-E of 40,570 t for 2023 to 2025. According to the EC protocol in Rec. 23-07 and section 19.12, no EC exists that would warrant deviating from the TAC calculated under the MP for 2025. Summary table: current reported catch (2023) 39,247 t as of September 2024; relative fishing mortality Fcurrent/F0.1 (2020) = 0.81 (0.48-1.62); stock status (2020): overfishing no; TAC 2023-2025 40,570 t. Footnote repeats that biomass reference points to determine stock status were not estimated since the 2017 assessment due to uncertainty in recruitment potential.

sources.title: ICCAT REPORT 2024-2025 (I)
sources.content: Contains the recommendations adopted in 2024, including the recommendation amending Rec. 22-08. Lines around pages 125-128 note the SCRS completed an MSE to establish a management procedure including harvest control rules, and the Commission decided on the MP at its 2022 annual meeting to establish TACs for 2023 and thereafter. Paragraph 4 of the recommendation states TACs, inclusive of dead discards, for 2023 to 2025 shall be set at 40,570 t in accordance with the MP, and TACs for 2026 and thereafter shall be decided at the 2025 Commission annual meeting in accordance with the MP.

sources.title: ICCAT 2025 Annual Meeting Press Release
sources.content: Press release for the 2025 annual meeting in Seville. States ICCAT agreed to set new TACs for western and eastern bluefin tuna, based on the current management procedure, and new quota allocations. The TACs for 2026-2028 were set at 3,081.6 t for the western stock and 48,403 t for the eastern Atlantic stock, corresponding to TAC increases of 13% and 19.3%, respectively. The release also states that in 2025 full scientific stock assessments were carried out for bigeye tuna, white marlin, and Atlantic shortfin mako shark, implying eastern bluefin TACs were set through the MP framework rather than a new 2025 full eastern-stock benchmark assessment.

query: What is the current deforestation rate in the Brazilian Cerrado biome, what are the main commodity drivers, and what specific enforcement actions has IBAMA taken in the last two years?
synthesis:
Current deforestation rates in the Brazilian Cerrado show a significant downward trend after years of persistent increases. For the 2025 reporting period, the deforestation rate was 7,235.27 km², an 11.49% reduction from the previous year and the lowest rate in five years [Deforestation falls by 11% in Amazon and Cerrado in a year | WWF Brasil](https://www.wwf.org.br/en/?93221/Deforestation-falls-by-11-in-Amazon-and-Cerrado-in-a-year). Other reports indicate a 25.7% drop in 2024, though the biome remains the most deforested in Brazil, even exceeding the Amazon [In one year, deforestation and conversion falls 30.6% in the Amazon ...](https://www.wwf.org.br/en/?90200/In-one-year-deforestation-falls-306-in-the-Amazon-and-257-in-the-Cerrado) [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/). The Matopiba region (Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia) remains the primary hotspot, accounting for 75% of Cerrado deforestation in 2024 [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/).

Main commodity drivers include:
- **Soy Production:** Identified as the primary driver of land clearing, often linked to large-scale producers and international traders like Cargill and Bunge [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/) [Why has the Brazilian Cerrado been left behind by voluntary environmental policies?](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0959378025000421).
- **Cattle Ranching:** Expansion of pasture for beef production is a major direct driver of habitat conversion [Brazilian beef exports and deforestation - Insights - trase.earth](https://trase.earth/insights/brazilian-beef-exports-and-deforestation) [Drivers of Deforestation - Our World in Data](https://ourworldindata.org/drivers-of-deforestation).

IBAMA enforcement actions between 2023 and 2025 have focused on institutional reactivation and financial pressure:
- **Operation Caixa-Forte (2025):** Targeted the financial sector, fining three banks over R$ 3.63 million for granting rural credit to properties already under environmental embargoes in the MATOPIBA region [The Liability of Financial Institutions in Brazil for Environmental Violations: Reflections from Operation Caixa-Forte](https://www.felsberg.com.br/en/the-liability-of-financial-institutions-in-brazil-for-environmental-violations-reflections-from-operation-caixa-forte/).
- **PPCerrado:** The launch of the "Action Plan for the Prevention and Control of Deforestation and Fires in the Cerrado" to coordinate conservation efforts [Deforestation falls by 11% in Amazon and Cerrado in a year | WWF Brasil](https://www.wwf.org.br/en/?93221/Deforestation-falls-by-11-in-Amazon-and-Cerrado-in-a-year).
- **Rural Credit Restrictions:** Implementation of rules prohibiting credit for businesses on illegally deforested land [Deforestation falls by 11% in Amazon and Cerrado in a year | WWF Brasil](https://www.wwf.org.br/en/?93221/Deforestation-falls-by-11-in-Amazon-and-Cerrado-in-a-year).
- **Field Enforcement:** As of early 2024, IBAMA reported 6,907 hectares embargoed, US$ 12.2 million in fines, and US$ 19.2 million in seized assets related to environmental violations [Interview with the President of Ibama, Brazil](https://agribrasilis.com/2024/02/27/ibama-brazil1/).

sources.title: Deforestation falls by 11% in Amazon and Cerrado in a year | WWF Brasil
sources.content:
### Cerrado Deforestation Rates
- **Current Rate (2025 Reporting Period):** 7,235.27 km², representing an 11.49% reduction from the previous year. This is the lowest rate in five years.
- **Trend:** Deforestation in the Cerrado had been increasing persistently until 2024. The cumulative reduction between 2023 and 2025 reached 34.29%.
- **Regional Statistics (MATOPIBA Region):** 
  - **Tocantins:** 1,489 km² (down 26.20%)
  - **Maranhão:** 2,006 km² (down 19.30%)
  - **Piauí:** 1,350 km² (up 33.10%)
  - **Bahia:** 790 km² (up 9.30%)

### Main Commodity Drivers
- **Agricultural Expansion:** The report identifies a direct relationship between the expansion of commodity production and environmental destruction, specifically in the MATOPIBA region (the country's newest agricultural frontier).
- **Soy Production:** Soy is highlighted as a primary commodity driver. The "Soy Moratorium" is cited as a critical tool for reducing deforestation footprints, though it faces legislative attacks. The productive sector is urged to eliminate deforestation from commodity production chains and implement sustainable financial mechanisms.

### Enforcement and Government Actions (2023–2025)
Since 2023, Brazil has implemented several measures to control devastation through the resumption of enforcement and the reactivation of environmental control bodies (such as IBAMA) that were previously dismantled:
- **PPCerrado:** Launch of the "Action Plan for the Prevention and Control of Deforestation and Fires in the Cerrado."
- **Federal Secretariat:** Creation of a specific federal secretariat dedicated to deforestation control.
- **Rural Credit Restrictions:** Expansion of rural credit programs that include environmental restrictions, limiting funds for properties associated with illegal deforestation.
- **Reactivation of Governance:** Restoration of environmental control and governance bodies that had been subjected to dismantling between 2018 and 2022.
- **PPCDAM:** Resumption of the Plan for the Prevention and Control of Deforestation in the Amazon, which contributed to a 4-gigaton reduction in CO2 emissions.
- **Tropical Forest Forever Facility (TFFF):** A proposed financial mechanism to raise funds for tropical forest protection, aiming to allocate at least 20% of resources to indigenous peoples.

sources.title: Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming
sources.content:
### Current Deforestation Rate in the Cerrado
- **2024 Figures:** The Cerrado lost **652,197 hectares (1.61 million acres)** of native vegetation in 2024.
- **Status:** For the second consecutive year, the Cerrado was the most deforested biome in Brazil, exceeding the Amazon's deforestation rates.
- **Historical Trend:** Agricultural activity in the biome expanded by **74% between 1985 and 2024**.
- **Regional Hotspot:** The **Matopiba** region—comprising the states of Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia—accounted for **75%** of all Cerrado deforestation in 2024.
- **Remaining Vegetation:** Only slightly more than half of the Cerrado is currently covered by native vegetation.

### Main Commodity Drivers
- **Soy Production:** The primary driver of land clearing is agricultural expansion for soy. Large-scale producers such as **Mizote Group** and **Franciosi Agro** were specifically identified for clearing **986 hectares (2,436 acres)** since May 2024 and **23,164 hectares (57,239 acres)** between January 2021 and April 2024 in western Bahia.
- **International Supply Chains:** Major commodity traders **Cargill and Bunge** are responsible for approximately **95% of soy exported to the EU** from western Bahia. This soy enters international supply chains, including chicken sold by McDonald’s and European supermarkets.
- **Regulatory Gaps:** The European Union’s antideforestation regulation (EUDR) currently excludes approximately **74% of the Cerrado** because it is classified as "other wooded land" rather than forest under FAO definitions.

### Enforcement and Accountability Actions
While the text does not explicitly detail federal IBAMA actions, it highlights specific state-level and private-sector enforcement:
- **Certification Suspensions:** The **Roundtable on Responsible Soy (RTRS)** suspended the certificates of **Franciosi Agro and Horita Group** in 2024 following an Earthsight investigation into illegal clearing and allegations of violence/land grabbing.
- **Illegal Clearing Identification:** An investigation by Earthsight was unable to identify deforestation permits for **60%** of the 23,164 hectares cleared by Mizote and Franciosi between 2021 and 2024, suggesting illegal activity.
- **State Agency Involvement:** Clearances were cross-referenced with authorizations from **Inema** (the Bahia state environmental agency), though some clearing occurred without these permits.
- **Soy Moratorium Crisis:** The article notes the potential **dismantling of the Soy Moratorium** (a voluntary agreement to curb soy-related deforestation) which is slated for suspension in 2026, further threatening enforcement efforts.

sources.title: Why has the Brazilian Cerrado been left behind by voluntary environmental policies?
sources.content:
The provided document discusses the environmental policy landscape of the Brazilian Cerrado, specifically focusing on why it has historically been excluded from zero-deforestation agreements like the Soy Moratorium.

- **Commodity Drivers**: The expansion of soy production is explicitly identified as a primary deforestation driver in the Brazilian Cerrado. While the 2006 Soy Moratorium successfully reduced soy-driven deforestation in the Amazon, the Cerrado remains largely unprotected from this expansion.
- **Deforestation Rate and Trends**: The article notes that the Cerrado has experienced "substantial soy deforestation" and characterizes the biome as being "overlooked in environmental policies" compared to the Amazon. However, specific numerical rates for the current year or the last two years are not provided in this abstract.
- **Policy and Enforcement Context**: The text identifies a "new political window" for Cerrado conservation following Brazil’s shift to a "left-centre coalition" (referring to the current administration). It mentions ongoing efforts to extend "new due-diligence deforestation regulations" to include the Cerrado and other wooded lands. 
- **Institutional/Political Factors**: The failure to adopt protection policies in the Cerrado is attributed to differences in public awareness, national politics, narratives, changes in trade relationships, and sunk investments compared to the Amazon region.

sources.title: The Liability of Financial Institutions in Brazil for Environmental Violations: Reflections from Operation Caixa-Forte
sources.content:
On March 19, 2025, the Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA) launched Operation Caixa-Forte, a significant enforcement action specifically targeting the financial sector's role in Cerrado deforestation. 

Key Enforcement Actions:
- Operation Caixa-Forte focuses on inspecting and penalizing financial institutions that granted rural credit to properties already embargoed for illegal deforestation within the Cerrado biome.
- IBAMA has fined three banks located in the states of Maranhão, Tocantins, and Piauí (part of the MATOPIBA region).
- Total fines issued as of the report date exceed R$ 3.63 million.
- Potential additional sanctions for these institutions include the suspension or prohibition of new financing operations and the cancellation of environmental licenses.
- The operation utilizes a cross-reference of databases, including rural property records, vegetation suppression authorizations, and rural credit operation data to identify illegal financing.

Legal and Regulatory Context:
- Under Brazilian law (specifically Articles 7 and 8 of Complementary Law No. 140/2011), rural credit is strictly prohibited for businesses located on properties with active federal or state environmental embargoes resulting from the economic use of illegally deforested areas.
- Financial institutions are required to consult the Environmental Infractions and Embargoes Register maintained by IBAMA before granting credit to ensure properties are compliant.
- Non-compliant financial institutions may also face sanctions from the Central Bank of Brazil for violating financial regulations related to environmental risk.

While the document highlights the enforcement focus on the Cerrado biome and the states of Maranhão, Tocantins, and Piauí, it does not provide specific annual percentage deforestation rates or a list of commodity drivers (e.g., soy, cattle) beyond the general context of 'rural credit' and 'economic use of illegally deforested areas.'

query: What is the current deforestation rate in the Brazilian Cerrado biome, what are the main commodity drivers, and what specific enforcement actions has IBAMA taken in the last two years?
synthesis:
The most current **official annual deforestation rate** for the Brazilian Cerrado is **8,174 km²** for the period **August 2023–July 2024**, according to INPE/PRODES. The federal government and IBAMA describe this as a **25.7% decline** from the previous annual period and the **lowest level since 2019**. [Dados do Prodes apontam redução no desmatamento na Amazônia e no Cerrado brasileiros](https://www.gov.br/inpe/pt-br/assuntos/ultimas-noticias/dados-do-prodes-apontam-reducao-no-desmatamento-na-amazonia-e-no-cerrado-brasileiros-1) [Desmatamento caiu na Amazônia e no Cerrado em 2024](https://www.gov.br/secom/pt-br/fatos/brasil-contra-fake/noticias/2025/05/desmatamento-caiu-na-amazonia-e-no-cerrado-em-2024) [Desmatamento cai 30,6% na Amazônia e 25,7% no Cerrado em 2024](https://www.gov.br/mma/pt-br/composicao/smc/departamento-de-instrumentos-de-mercado-e-redd/redd/central-de-conteudos/noticias/desmatamento-cai-30-6-na-amazonia-e-25-7-no-cerrado-em-2024)

If you want a **more real-time indicator** rather than the official annual PRODES rate, IBAMA reported that **between August 2024 and May 2025** enforcement/monitoring data still showed the Cerrado on a downward trajectory, while government communications also reported shorter-term DETER alert declines in late 2024. These are not the same metric as the annual PRODES rate, so they should be treated as interim monitoring signals rather than the official yearly rate. [No mês de maio, Pantanal e Cerrado registram continuidade na queda no desmatamento; Amazônia é afetada por incêndios e seca histórica](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/no-mes-de-maio-pantanal-e-cerrado-registram-continuidade-na-queda-no-desmatamento-amazonia-e-afetada-por-incendios-e-seca-historica) [Deforestation drops 77.2% in Pantanal, 57.2% in Cerrado between August-November 2024](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2024/12/deforestation-drops-77-2-in-pantanal-57-2-in-cerrado-between-august-november-2024)

On **main commodity drivers**, the strongest official statement I found is from IBAMA: Cerrado deforestation is **directly associated with the expansion of large-scale agriculture and livestock**, and **soy is the crop with the greatest impact in Matopiba**, the biome’s newest agricultural frontier. IBAMA also states that **Matopiba accounted for 48% of all native vegetation cleared in the previous year**. [Ibama realiza operação para fiscalizar bancos por financiamento em áreas desmatadas ilegalmente](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/ibama-realiza-operacao-para-fiscalizar-bancos-por-financiamento-em-areas-desmatadas-ilegalmente)

A second IBAMA enforcement notice gives more specificity on land use after illegal clearing in Matopiba: inspectors found embargoed areas continuing to generate income through **sales of soybeans, corn, beans, and other agricultural crops**, including **sale and leasing of embargoed land**. This supports a commodity profile led by **soy**, with broader row-crop expansion and **cattle/livestock** also identified by IBAMA as major drivers. [Desmatamento ilegal no Cerrado gera R$ 206 milhões em multas por descumprimento de embargos e impedimento da regeneração natural — Ibama](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/desmatamento-ilegal-no-cerrado-gera-r-206-milhoes-em-multas-por-descumprimento-de-embargos-e-impedimento-da-regeneracao-natural) [Ibama realiza operação para fiscalizar bancos por financiamento em áreas desmatadas ilegalmente](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/ibama-realiza-operacao-para-fiscalizar-bancos-por-financiamento-em-areas-desmatadas-ilegalmente)

On **what IBAMA has specifically done in the last two years** in the Cerrado, the clearest documented actions I found are:

1. **Operation Nova Fronteira** in **Matopiba** (reported **December 2, 2024**, covering actions in **October and November 2024**): IBAMA said it issued **52 violation notices**, imposed about **R$206 million in environmental fines**, and **seized 23 pieces of equipment and machinery** such as tractors and sprayers. The operation targeted **illegal deforestation** and especially **noncompliance with prior embargoes**. IBAMA said technical analysis of satellite imagery showed embargoed areas were still being economically exploited. [Desmatamento ilegal no Cerrado gera R$ 206 milhões em multas por descumprimento de embargos e impedimento da regeneração natural — Ibama](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/desmatamento-ilegal-no-cerrado-gera-r-206-milhoes-em-multas-por-descumprimento-de-embargos-e-impedimento-da-regeneracao-natural)

2. **Operation Caixa-Forte** (reported **March 19, 2025**): IBAMA launched an enforcement line focused on **banks that extended rural credit to farms embargoed for illegal deforestation in the Cerrado**, in violation of environmental rules and Brazil’s rural credit rules. IBAMA said that, at that point, it had issued **10 violation notices**, totaling **more than R$3.63 million in fines**, against **three banks** in **Maranhão, Tocantins, and Piauí**. It identified illegal financing linked to **seven rural properties** and **240 hectares of embargoed areas**. The benefited landowners were also cited for **breach of embargo** and **impeding natural regeneration**. [Ibama realiza operação para fiscalizar bancos por financiamento em áreas desmatadas ilegalmente](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/ibama-realiza-operacao-para-fiscalizar-bancos-por-financiamento-em-areas-desmatadas-ilegalmente)

3. **Broad Cerrado enforcement totals** reported by IBAMA for **August 2024–May 2025**: **R$578 million in fines**, **737 infraction notices**, **455 embargo orders**, **376 seizure terms** involving **R$54.9 million in seized goods**, **77 destruction orders**, **370 preventive fire notifications**, and **20 civil actions**. This is a biome-wide enforcement summary rather than one named operation, but it is the most concrete recent aggregate picture of IBAMA’s activity I found. [No mês de maio, Pantanal e Cerrado registram continuidade na queda no desmatamento; Amazônia é afetada por incêndios e seca histórica](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/no-mes-de-maio-pantanal-e-cerrado-registram-continuidade-na-queda-no-desmatamento-amazonia-e-afetada-por-incendios-e-seca-historica)

4. **Longer two-year enforcement baseline**: on **National Cerrado Day** in **September 2024**, IBAMA said that in **2023 and 2024** it had issued **2,768 infraction notices for illegal deforestation in the Cerrado**. The same IBAMA notice, citing PPCerrado, said **at least half of Cerrado deforestation is estimated to occur without authorization**. [No Dia Nacional do Cerrado, Ibama combate incêndios florestais e desmatamento ilegal no bioma — Ibama](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/no-dia-nacional-do-cerrado-ibama-combate-incendios-florestais-e-desmatamento-ilegal-no-bioma)

**Bottom line:** the current official annual Cerrado deforestation rate is **8,174 km²/year** for **August 2023–July 2024**. The main commodity drivers identified in official sources are **large-scale agriculture and livestock**, with **soy as the dominant crop driver in Matopiba**, alongside other row crops such as **corn and beans**. Over the last two years, IBAMA’s documented Cerrado actions include **Operation Nova Fronteira** (R$206 million in fines; 52 notices; 23 machines seized), **Operation Caixa-Forte** (10 notices; >R$3.63 million in fines against three banks; seven financed properties; 240 embargoed hectares), and broader **August 2024–May 2025 biome-wide enforcement** totaling **R$578 million in fines**, **737 notices**, and **455 embargoes**. [Dados do Prodes apontam redução no desmatamento na Amazônia e no Cerrado brasileiros](https://www.gov.br/inpe/pt-br/assuntos/ultimas-noticias/dados-do-prodes-apontam-reducao-no-desmatamento-na-amazonia-e-no-cerrado-brasileiros-1) [Ibama realiza operação para fiscalizar bancos por financiamento em áreas desmatadas ilegalmente](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/ibama-realiza-operacao-para-fiscalizar-bancos-por-financiamento-em-areas-desmatadas-ilegalmente) [Desmatamento ilegal no Cerrado gera R$ 206 milhões em multas por descumprimento de embargos e impedimento da regeneração natural — Ibama](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/desmatamento-ilegal-no-cerrado-gera-r-206-milhoes-em-multas-por-descumprimento-de-embargos-e-impedimento-da-regeneracao-natural) [No mês de maio, Pantanal e Cerrado registram continuidade na queda no desmatamento; Amazônia é afetada por incêndios e seca histórica](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/no-mes-de-maio-pantanal-e-cerrado-registram-continuidade-na-queda-no-desmatamento-amazonia-e-afetada-por-incendios-e-seca-historica)

One important caveat: official sources distinguish between **PRODES annual rates** and **DETER/other near-real-time alerts**. I found solid official data for the annual rate through **July 2024**, and interim enforcement/alert indicators into **2025**, but I did **not** find a newer official Cerrado annual PRODES rate beyond that in the sources I used for this answer.

sources.title: Dados do Prodes apontam redução no desmatamento na Amazônia e no Cerrado brasileiros — Instituto Nacional de Pesquisas Espaciais

For the period August 2023 to July 2024, the official deforestation rate in the Cerrado was 8,174 km². The notice says this was a reduction relative to the previous period and frames the number as official annual monitoring data used for conservation and public policy.

sources.title: Desmatamento cai 30,6% na Amazônia e 25,7% no Cerrado em 2024 — Ministério do Meio Ambiente e Mudança do Clima
sources.content: MMA notice reporting that the official deforestation rate in the Cerrado for August 2023 to July 2024 was 8,174 km², the lowest since 2019, and that this represented a 25.7% decline. Also links this reduction to federal policy efforts and the goal of zero deforestation by 2030.

sources.title: Ibama realiza operação para fiscalizar bancos por financiamento em áreas desmatadas ilegalmente — Ibama
sources.content: Published March 19, 2025. Announces Operation Caixa-Forte. Purpose: punish financial institutions that granted rural credit to farms embargoed for illegal deforestation in the Cerrado, contrary to environmental law and National Monetary Council rules. By the publication date, IBAMA had issued 10 infraction notices totaling more than R$3.63 million in fines against three banks in Maranhão, Tocantins, and Piauí. Illegal financing was identified in seven rural properties, involving 240 hectares of embargoed areas. Landowners benefiting from the credit were cited for embargo violations and obstructing natural regeneration. The article says the Cerrado deforestation level in 2024 was 8,174 km² after a 25% reduction, but continued enforcement is still necessary. It states Cerrado deforestation is directly associated with expansion of large-scale agriculture and livestock. It says soy is the crop with the greatest impact in Matopiba, the biome’s newest agricultural frontier, and that Matopiba accounted for 48% of all vegetation suppressed in the previous year. It says irregularities were identified by crossing deforestation data, vegetation suppression authorizations, rural property records, satellite imagery, and rural credit operations. Notes that further phases of the operation will occur.

sources.title: Desmatamento ilegal no Cerrado gera R$ 206 milhões em multas por descumprimento de embargos e impedimento da regeneração natural — Ibama
sources.content: Published December 2, 2024. Reports Operation Nova Fronteira in Matopiba, covering parts of Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia. In October and November 2024, the operation resulted in 52 enforcement actions/violation notices totaling approximately R$206 million in environmental fines. IBAMA also seized 23 pieces of equipment and machinery, including tractors and sprayers. Technical analysis of geospatial satellite imagery showed that previous embargoes imposed by IBAMA in inspected areas had not been respected. According to inspectors, violators had for years been obtaining illicit economic gains through sale of soybeans, corn, beans, and other agricultural crops, as well as sale and leasing of embargoed land. The region is described as a new agricultural frontier whose characteristics facilitate mechanization and establishment of annual crops at the expense of native vegetation. Financial damage to offenders from seizures was estimated at R$24 million.

sources.title: No mês de maio, Pantanal e Cerrado registram continuidade na queda no desmatamento; Amazônia é afetada por incêndios e seca histórica — Ibama
sources.content: IBAMA 2025 notice with enforcement totals. For the Cerrado, states that between August 2024 and May 2025 IBAMA applied R$578 million in fines, issued 737 infraction notices, 455 embargo terms, 376 seizure terms totaling R$54.9 million in seized goods, 77 destruction terms, 370 notifications for preventive fire measures, and 20 civil actions. Presents these as part of intensified federal inspection and accountability measures against illegal deforestation. sources.title: No Dia Nacional do Cerrado, Ibama combate incêndios florestais e desmatamento ilegal no bioma — Ibama

sources.title: Deforestation drops 77.2% in Pantanal, 57.2% in Cerrado between August-November 2024 — Secretaria de Comunicação Social

States that the Cerrado biome saw a 25.7% reduction between 2023 and 2024 in official annual terms and discusses federal actions such as integrating CAR and ASV data, environmental governance, and plans for controlling deforestation.
